# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark, methodology, failure-analysis, and repair-evaluation evidence about robustness failures in code-generation multi-agent systems under semantic-preserving input mutations, plus evidence about a proposed repair method and its measured effects.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: Lyu et al., abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Popular code-generation MASs failed on 7.9% to 83.3% of problems they had originally solved after semantic-preserving input mutations were applied.

## Source Excerpt

"Our findings reveal substantial robustness flaws of various popular MASs: they fail to solve 7.9%–83.3% of problems they initially resolved successfully after applying the semantic-preserving mutations."

## Note

This is an aggregate robustness result stated in the abstract and later supported by Table 2.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The fuzzing pipeline used four semantic-preserving mutation operators: Rephrase, Insert, Expand, and Condense.

## Source Excerpt

"Table 1 presents these four mutation operators: Rephrase, Insert, Expand, and Condense."

## Note

These operators modify expression style while aiming to preserve semantic meaning.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The fuzzing fitness function combined a code reward based on pass-rate differences and a plan reward based on Sentence-BERT plan similarity.

## Source Excerpt

"We propose a novel fitness function that calculates rewards for MAS outputs on mutated questions... across two distinct aspects: code and plan." Also: "We apply Sentence-BERT ... to obtain the embedding and calculate semantic similarity between corresponding plans." And: "The final fitness function is calculated as the sum of the code reward and the plan reward."

## Note

The source defines both reward formulas explicitly in Eqs. 1 and 2.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The evaluation covered three MASs, three backend LLMs, and four datasets.

## Source Excerpt

"We evaluate three widely-adopted code generation MASs: Self-Collaboration Code Generation ... MetaGPT ... and PairCoder." "We use three different LLMs as backend models ... GPT-3.5-Turbo ... GPT-4o ... and Deepseek-Coder-V2." "We employ four code generation datasets: HumanEval ET, MBPP ET, CodeContest and CoderEval."

## Note

This describes the experimental coverage, not the performance outcome.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study used Pass@10 as the primary robustness metric and ran each system 10 times per question.

## Source Excerpt

"We adopt Pass@k ... as our primary metric ... we set k = 10 in our evaluation." Also: "To measure Pass@10, we execute the system 10 times (n = 10). If the MAS fails in all 10 trials, we consider the problem unsolvable by the MAS."

## Note

The metric choice is intended to reduce the effect of occasional random failures.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Lyu et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The largest reported post-fuzzing drop in Table 2 was 83.3% for MetaGPT with Deepseek on CodeContest.

## Source Excerpt

In Table 2, the row "MetaGPT Deepseek" under "CodeContest" shows "0.0242 0.1515 ↓ 83.3%".

## Note

The abstract also reports the overall drop range ending at 83.3%.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Lyu et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The smallest reported post-fuzzing drop in Table 2 was 7.9% for PairCoder with GPT-4o on MBPP ET.

## Source Excerpt

In Table 2, the row "PairCoder GPT-4o" under "MBPP ET" shows "0.6791 0.7377 ↓ 7.9%".

## Note

The paper identifies this setting as the best robustness case in the evaluated table.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: claim-support
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 5 and Table 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In the sampled failure analysis, planner-coder gap accounted for 75.3% of failures, exceeding plan logic errors at 15.3% and invalid cases at 9.3%.

## Source Excerpt

"As presented in Table 3, this substantial gap between planners and coders constitutes the primary cause of failures, accounting for 75.3% of all failure cases. In contrast, plan logic errors account for only 15.3% of failures, and a small proportion of failures (9.3%) are categorized as invalid cases..."

## Note

This mechanism-relevant evidence is bounded because it comes from manual analysis of a random 20% sample of over 700 failures rather than the full failure set.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The failure-reason analysis examined a random 20% sample of more than 700 fuzzing failures and reported inter-rater agreement of Cohen's kappa = 0.88.

## Source Excerpt

"Specifically, we randomly sample 20% of over 700 failures discovered through fuzzing..." and "In our case, the score is 0.88, indicating a high level of agreement."

## Note

This supports the reliability of the failure taxonomy, but it remains bounded by sampling and manual categorization.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: claim-support
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper attributes planner-coder gap mainly to plans being logically correct but too brief or abstract, and to coders misinterpreting expressions or complex logic without detailed explanation.

## Source Excerpt

"Through comprehensive analysis, we identify two principal underlying causes of this gap: 1) although the plan is logically correct, it is typically brief and abstract, and may not clarify certain concepts and conditions for coders; 2) coders are vulnerable to certain expressions and complex logic if there are no detailed explanations."

## Note

This is mechanism-relevant because it states the proposed causal pathway for failure, but it carries tension because it is an interpretive causal claim derived from the authors' failure analysis rather than a controlled causal experiment.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 6.2-6.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The proposed repair method has two main components: multi-prompt generation and insertion of a monitor agent that performs plan interpretation and code check.

## Source Excerpt

"Our approach comprises two principal components: multi-prompt generation and monitor agent insertion." Also: "This new agent in the whole MAS pipeline performs two critical tasks: plan interpretation and code check."

## Note

This is a direct description of the intervention the paper evaluates.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: result
citation: Lyu et al., Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Across MAS, backend, and dataset settings, the repair method solved 40.0% to 88.9% of failures identified during fuzzing.

## Source Excerpt

"Table 4 presents the compelling repair results: our method enables MASs to successfully solve 40.0%–88.9% of the questions they previously failed."

## Note

This is the paper's headline repair-rate range for RQ1.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Lyu et al., Table 5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
For SCCG with GPT-3.5, the repair method solved 83.9% of planner-coder-gap failures and 0.0% of invalid cases.

## Source Excerpt

"Table 5 presents the repair results for different error patterns identified in SCCG (GPT-3.5)." The table reports "Overall" planner-coder-gap "Solved (%) 83.9" and "Invalid 0.0".

## Note

This evidence is bounded because it is reported for one MAS/backend slice rather than all evaluated configurations.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: result
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 7.3 and abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
After repair, re-running fuzzing showed failure-count reductions of up to 85.7%.

## Source Excerpt

"Re-execution of the fuzzing process on the repaired MASs shows that the number of found failures decreases up to 85.7%..." Also in Sec. 7.3: "PairCoder (GPT-4o) exhibits the most remarkable robustness improvement against fuzzing, with 85.7% of failures in HumanEval ET eliminated."

## Note

This is the strongest reported robustness-improvement result after applying the repair method.
---

In the sampled failure analysis, planner-coder gap accounted for 75.3% of failures, exceeding plan logic errors at 15.3% and invalid cases at 9.3%.

"As presented in Table 3, this substantial gap between planners and coders constitutes the primary cause of failures, accounting for 75.3% of all failure cases. ## Note

This mechanism-relevant evidence is bounded because it comes from manual analysis of a random 20% sample of over 700 failures rather than the full failure set.
```

The failure-reason analysis examined a random 20% sample of more than 700 fuzzing failures and reported inter-rater agreement of Cohen's kappa = 0.88.

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: claim-support
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper attributes planner-coder gap mainly to plans being logically correct but too brief or abstract, and to coders misinterpreting expressions or complex logic without detailed explanation.

## Source Excerpt

"Through comprehensive analysis, we identify two principal underlying causes of this gap: 1) although the plan is logically correct, it is typically brief and abstract, and may not clarify certain concepts and conditions for coders; 2) coders are vulnerable to certain expressions and complex logic if there are no detailed explanations."

## Note

This is mechanism-relevant because it states the proposed causal pathway for failure, but it carries tension because it is an interpretive causal claim derived from the authors' failure analysis rather than a controlled causal experiment.
```

## Note

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark, methodology, failure-analysis, and repair-evaluation evidence about robustness failures in code-generation multi-agent systems under semantic-preserving input mutations, plus evidence about a proposed repair method and its measured effects.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: Lyu et al., abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Popular code-generation MASs failed on 7.9% to 83.3% of problems they had originally solved after semantic-preserving input mutations were applied.

## Source Excerpt

"Our findings reveal substantial robustness flaws of various popular MASs: they fail to solve 7.9%–83.3% of problems they initially resolved successfully after applying the semantic-preserving mutations."

## Note

This is an aggregate robustness result stated in the abstract and later supported by Table 2.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The fuzzing pipeline used four semantic-preserving mutation operators: Rephrase, Insert, Expand, and Condense.

## Source Excerpt

"Table 1 presents these four mutation operators: Rephrase, Insert, Expand, and Condense."

## Note

These operators modify expression style while aiming to preserve semantic meaning.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The fuzzing fitness function combined a code reward based on pass-rate differences and a plan reward based on Sentence-BERT plan similarity.

## Source Excerpt

"We propose a novel fitness function that calculates rewards for MAS outputs on mutated questions... across two distinct aspects: code and plan." Also: "We apply Sentence-BERT ... to obtain the embedding and calculate semantic similarity between corresponding plans." And: "The final fitness function is calculated as the sum of the code reward and the plan reward."

## Note

The source defines both reward formulas explicitly in Eqs. 1 and 2.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-04

citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The evaluation covered three MASs, three backend LLMs, and four datasets.

## Source Excerpt

"We evaluate three widely-adopted code generation MASs: Self-Collaboration Code Generation ... MetaGPT ... and PairCoder." "We use three different LLMs as backend models ... GPT-3.5-Turbo ... GPT-4o ... and Deepseek-Coder-V2." "We employ four code generation datasets: HumanEval ET, MBPP ET, CodeContest and CoderEval."

## Note

This describes the experimental coverage, not the performance outcome.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-024
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Lyu et al., Sec. 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.10460
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study used Pass@10 as the primary robustness metric and ran each system 10 times per question.

## Source Excerpt

"We adopt Pass@k ... as our primary metric ... we set k = 10 in our evaluation." Also: "To measure Pass@10, we execute the system 10 times (n = 10). If the MAS fails in all 10 trials, we consider the problem unsolvable by the MAS."

## Note

The metric choice is intended to reduce the effect of occasional random failures.
```
